was the 109th season of professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in Australia and the 19th season run by the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League"). The season started in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") with the annual [Auckland Nines](/wiki/2016_NRL_Auckland_Nines "2016 NRL Auckland Nines"), and was followed by the [All Stars Match](/wiki/2016_All_Stars_match "2016 All Stars match"), which was played at [Suncorp Stadium](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") in Brisbane,[[1]](#cite_note-AllStars16-1) and the [World Club Series](/wiki/2016_World_Club_Series "2016 World Club Series"). The season concluded on October 2 with the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") winning their first ever premiership after 50 seasons, having entered the competition in 1967.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The season was also noteworthy as it was the first time that all three Queensland based teams made the finals.

The **2016 NRL season** was the 109th season of professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in Australia and the 19th season run by the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League"). The season started in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") with the annual [Auckland Nines](/wiki/2016_NRL_Auckland_Nines "2016 NRL Auckland Nines"), and was followed by the [All Stars Match](/wiki/2016_All_Stars_match "2016 All Stars match"), which was played at [Suncorp Stadium](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") in Brisbane,[[1]](#cite_note-AllStars16-1) and the [World Club Series](/wiki/2016_World_Club_Series "2016 World Club Series"). The season concluded on October 2 with the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks") winning their first ever premiership after 50 seasons, having entered the competition in 1967.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The season was also noteworthy as it was the first time that all three Queensland based teams made the finals.

## Teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_NRL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Teams")]

The lineup of teams remained unchanged for the 10th consecutive year. The NRL's salary cap for the clubs' top 25 players was $A6.3M for 2016.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Pre-season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_NRL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-season")]

The 2016 pre-season featured the third edition of the [Auckland Nines](/wiki/Auckland_Nines "Auckland Nines") competition, held over a weekend at Auckland's [Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park") in which the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") defeated the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") in the final. The [All Stars match](/wiki/2016_All_Stars_match "2016 All Stars match") was held on February 13 at [Suncorp Stadium](/wiki/Lang_Park "Lang Park") in Brisbane. The [2016 World Club Series](/wiki/2016_World_Club_Series "2016 World Club Series") took place in England with the [NRL premiers](/wiki/2015_NRL_season "2015 NRL season") [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys") defeating the [Super League champions](/wiki/Super_League_XX "Super League XX") [Leeds Rhinos](/wiki/Leeds_Rhinos "Leeds Rhinos") in the [World Club Challenge](/wiki/World_Club_Challenge "World Club Challenge") match.

## Parramatta Eels salary cap breach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_NRL_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Parramatta Eels salary cap breach")]

Main article: [Parramatta Eels salary cap breach](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels_salary_cap_breach "Parramatta Eels salary cap breach")

The [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") were faced with starting the season on -4 points due to salary cap indiscretions in 2015, however the NRL was satisfied with governance changes at the Eels and no points were deducted.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

However, on 3 May 2016, after further salary cap breaches were exposed by the NRL, CEO Todd Greenberg announced the preliminary penalties of the club being fined $1 million, deducted twelve competition points, stripped of its [2016 Auckland Nines](/wiki/2016_Auckland_Nines "2016 Auckland Nines") title[[14]](#cite_note-14) and they were barred from receiving any further competition points until they fell under the salary cap, which they were reported to have exceeded by $570,000. Five officials (chairman [Steve Sharp](/wiki/Steve_Sharp_(rugby_league) "Steve Sharp (rugby league)"), deputy chairman Tom Issa, director Peter Serrao, chief executive John Boulous and football manager [Daniel Anderson](/wiki/Daniel_Anderson_(rugby_league) "Daniel Anderson (rugby league)")) were also suspended indefinitely. On 12 May the NRL reported that Parramatta had fallen back under the salary cap for 2016 and were able to play for competition points again for their next scheduled game.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

On 9 July, after over 2 months of club officials contesting the preliminary penalties, Parramatta were handed their punishment with the addition of their for/against points tally accumulated from rounds 1-9 being deducted.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Finals series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2016_NRL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Finals series")]

See also: [2016 NRL season results - Finals series](/wiki/2016_NRL_season_results#Finals_series "2016 NRL season results")

A new format of extra time was introduced for the finals series where two additional 5-minute periods are played and if the scores are still tied afterwards, the next team to score wins.

The 2016 Finals series is notable as the first time three Queensland teams have all made the finals in one season.

Canberra also broke a long preliminary final drought reaching their first grand final qualifier in 19 years.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
